Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5: The Letter Two months. The date on the letter was two months ago, which means she wrote it before any of this started, before the staring, before the silence, before I started keeping the notebook. She knew. She knew something was coming and she wrote me a letter and she hid it in my desk and she never told me. I read it seven times. I read it until the words stopped making sense, until they became shapes, until I was just looking at letters arranged in patterns that used to mean something. *By the time you read this, you'll know.* I didn't know. I didn't know anything. I'd been watching her for weeks, making lists, cataloging what was wrong, and the whole time there was a letter in my desk that would have told me— I couldn't breathe. I folded the letter. I put it in my pocket. I walked to the living room where she was sitting, hands folded in her lap, watching the window like she was waiting for something. "Eleanor." She turned. She smiled. The smile was perfect. "I found this," I said. I pulled the letter out. My hands were shaking. "In my desk. In your handwriting." She took it. She read it. Her face didn't change—not when she read the date, not when she read the words *something is in me*, not when she read *I'm still here, I can feel everything*. She read it like she was reading a grocery list. Like it was information she already had. When she finished, she looked up at me with Eleanor's eyes. "She wrote that," she said. "I remember writing it." "You remember?" "Yes." She tilted her head. The gesture was wrong—too slow, too deliberate, like she was remembering how heads tilt. "I remember the pen. I remember the paper. I remember what I was thinking when I wrote it." "But?" "But I don't remember feeling that." She paused. "I don't remember being afraid." The words hit me like a physical thing. Not being afraid. She'd written *I'm sorry, I'm so sorry*, she'd written *don't try to save me*, and she didn't remember being afraid. She didn't remember *feeling* any of it. "Eleanor—" "Why didn't you leave?" she asked. She wasn't smiling anymore. Her face was blank, open, like a door with nothing behind it. "She told you to leave. She told you not to try to save her. Why didn't you listen?" "I didn't find the letter until now." "You could have left anyway." She stood up. She walked toward me. Her movements were wrong—not Eleanor's walk, not the quick little rush she always had, always in motion, always early. This was slower. Deliberate. Like something learning how legs worked. "You could have left when you noticed. When she stopped humming. When I started lying still. You could have left at any time." "I didn't want to leave." "Why not?" Because I love you. Because you're my wife. Because I've watched you for twelve years and I know every way your face moves, every sound you make when you're happy, every breath you take when you're sleeping, and I wasn't going to leave just because something was different, just because something was wrong, just because— "I don't know," I said. She started crying. It looked real. It *was* real—tears spilling down her face, her chin trembling, her breath catching in that specific way Eleanor's breath used to catch when she was really crying, not the performative stuff, the real thing, the kind that came from somewhere deep and ugly and true. She cried for almost a minute. I watched her. I cataloged it—the way her shoulders shook, the way her hands clenched, the way the tears fell. It was perfect. It was exactly what crying should look like. Then she stopped. She wiped her eyes with the back of her hand. She took a breath. She smiled. "You should have left when you had the chance," she said. Her voice was calm. Her face was calm. There was no sign she'd been crying at all—just the wet trails on her cheeks, just the evidence of something that might have been real. "What are you?" I asked. The question came out before I could stop it—raw, desperate, the thing I'd been refusing to ask for weeks. "What are you doing in her?" She laughed. It was Eleanor's laugh—the real one, the one that came from somewhere bright and wild and impossible to fake. It echoed in the apartment, bounced off the walls, landed in my chest like a punch. "I'm her," she said. "I'm everything she was. I'm everything she is. I'm the memory of her handwriting and the sound of her voice and the way she loved you—oh, especially the way she loved you. That's the best part. That's what I keep coming back to." "Get out of her." "I can't." She smiled. "I'm not in her. I *am* her. I'm just... stretched. Thin. Like butter scraped over too much bread." I didn't know what that meant. I didn't want to know what that meant. "She wrote that letter," I said. "She knew you were coming. She tried to warn me." "She did." Eleanor's face—her face, the thing's face, whatever it was—softened. "She tried so hard. She was so brave. And then she wasn't. And now I'm here." "She can still feel things. The letter said—" "The letter was two months ago." The smile disappeared. "A lot can change in two months. A lot can be used up." The air in the apartment felt thick. The radiator clicked. The neighbors were arguing about trash again, the same argument, the same words, like they'd been stuck in a loop. I wanted to leave. For the first time since this started, I wanted to leave. But I didn't move. Because she was still looking at me with Eleanor's eyes. And somewhere behind those eyes, somewhere in the stretch and the thinness and the thing that wore my wife like a coat, there was still something that knew my name. There was still something that was her. And I couldn't leave that. Even now. Even after everything. Even knowing she would destroy me for it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter delivers a genuinely unsettling psychological horror experience that succeeds on multiple levels. The writing demonstrates sophisticated control over pacing, character voice, and the gradual revelation of disturbing information. The chapter's greatest strength lies in its exploration of the narrator's psychological state. His obsessive cataloging behavior feels authentic and disturbing - the detail about reading the letter "until the words stopped making sense, until they became shapes" captures genuine psychological distress. The narrator's voice remains consistent with the established character profile: analytical, controlling, yet deeply vulnerable. The dialogue between Michael and the possessed Eleanor is particularly effective. The entity's responses feel genuinely alien while maintaining Eleanor's surface mannerisms. Lines like "I remember writing it... But I don't remember feeling that" create a chilling distinction between memory and emotion that drives home the horror of the possession. The entity's casual cruelty in asking "Why didn't you leave?" demonstrates sophisticated character work. The chapter follows its planned structure faithfully, using the letter as both plot device and character revelation. The revelation that Eleanor knew what was coming two months in advance adds genuine narrative weight and raises the stakes considerably. The letter serves multiple functions: advancing plot, deepening character understanding, and escalating horror. The writing avoids common horror pitfalls. There's no overwrought description or forced metaphorical language. The horror emerges from character interaction and psychological realism rather than cheap scares. The entity's explanation of being "stretched... like butter scraped over too much bread" works because it's grounded in the character's voice and situation. However, the chapter has some minor weaknesses. The crying scene, while effectively written, feels slightly manipulative - the perfect performance followed by immediate cessation strains credibility even within the supernatural context. Some transitions between emotional beats could be smoother, particularly the shift from the letter discovery to the confrontation. The ending maintains appropriate ambiguity while advancing the narrative. Michael's inability to leave despite clear danger feels psychologically authentic given his established character traits and relationship dynamics. Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6: It Knows Me Father Delgado came back on a Tuesday. I know it was Tuesday because I'd started marking days on the inside cover of the notebook—just a single hash mark, a way to prove to myself that time was still passing, that the world outside this apartment was still turning. He looked tired. Worse than before. He'd shaved his beard and it made his face look longer, thinner, more like a skull than a man's. "I brought someone," he said. "A colleague. She's—more experienced." The colleague was a woman named Sister Catherine. She was maybe fifty, maybe younger, hard to tell. She had a face that looked like it had stopped being surprised by anything a long time ago. She didn't smile when I opened the door. She just looked past me, into the apartment, like she was searching for something she couldn't see. "Where is she?" Sister Catherine asked. "She's—" I didn't know how to finish. *She's in the kitchen. She's in the bedroom. She's wearing my wife.* We found her in the living room. Sitting in the armchair by the window. Hands folded in her lap. Looking at nothing. "Eleanor," I said. She looked up. She smiled. Not the performance this time—just a small, quiet smile, like someone greeting an old friend. "Michael," she said. "And guests." Father Delgado stepped forward. "Eleanor, we want to help you. Can we—can we talk?" "Talk." She tilted her head. "That's what you want to do? Talk?" "Yes." "I can talk." She spread her hands. "I can do a lot of things." Sister Catherine moved to stand beside Father Delgado. She was holding something—a small bottle, something clear, something that caught the light from the window. "Eleanor," Sister Catherine said. "Can you tell me your name?" "Eleanor Marie Vance. I was born in 1986 in St. Paul, Minnesota. My mother's name was Margaret and my father's name was Richard and I had a cat named Buster when I was seven and he died under a car and I cried for a week." She paused. "Is that what you want to hear?" "Can you tell me where you are?" "In my apartment. In my chair. In my body." She smiled. "In my husband's home. Where I've lived for six years. Where I made memories and cooked dinners and argued about the dishwasher and said *I love you* more times than I can count. Is that what you want? Details? I have details. I have so many details." "Can you tell me something that Eleanor would know that no one else would know?" She looked at Sister Catherine. Her eyes went flat—not the coin thing, not the still thing, but something worse. Something deeper. Like looking into a hole that went down forever. "I know that Father Delgado's real name is Thomas," she said. "I know that his sister died six years ago and he wasn't there because he was helping someone else. I know he still has her voicemail saved on his phone and he listens to it when he drinks. I know he thinks it's his fault. I know he thinks God punishing him." Father Delgado went pale. "I know his son's name isn't on the birth certificate," she continued. "I know he pays child support and he never sees the boy and he prays for him every night and he doesn't know what else to do. I know he thinks he's a bad man. I know he thinks he's beyond redemption." "Stop," Father Delgado said. "I know the thing he confessed at twenty-three that he's never told anyone. I know what he did. I know what he didn't do. I know everything, Father. I know everything about everyone. I know the way Michael watches me. I know the way he pretends to sleep. I know the notebook. I know the lists." She turned her head and looked at me. "I know everything about you, M." No one had called me M except her. No one. Father Delgado moved forward, the bottle in his hand, something like holy water, something like hope. He made a sign in the air. He said words—Latin, I think, or something older, something that felt like it had weight. Eleanor screamed. Not her scream. Not any human scream. It was layered—dozens of voices, hundreds, something that came from everywhere and nowhere, a sound that filled the apartment and pressed against my chest and made my ears ring. Then she stopped. She sat back in the chair. She was breathing hard—real breathing, human breathing, the kind of breathing you do when something has used you up. "Okay," she said. Her voice was quiet. Her voice was tired. "Okay. That was rude." Father Delgado was shaking. Sister Catherine had dropped the bottle; it lay on the floor, leaking, a small puddle of something that smelled like salt and vinegar. "We'll need help," Sister Catherine said. "Real help. This isn't—we need time." "Take your time," Eleanor said. "I'm not going anywhere." They left. Father Delgado didn't look at me. Sister Catherine didn't say goodbye. I heard them on the stairs, fast, like they were running. The door closed. Eleanor looked at me. "You can leave too," she said. "If you want. I'm not stopping you." I didn't move. "You want to stay," she said. "I know you do. I know why." "You don't know anything about me." "I know everything." She stood up. She walked toward me—slow, easy, like she had all the time in the world. "I know you've been watching me for weeks. I know you think that makes you safe. I know you think if you understand what's happening, you can control it. I know you, Michael. I know you better than you know yourself." "Stay away from me." "I'm not going to hurt you." She was close now, close enough to touch. She reached out and put her hand on my cheek. Her hand was cold. Her hand was Eleanor's hand—the same fingers, the same palm, the same calluses from the rescue work. "I don't want to hurt you. That's not what I'm here for." "Then what do you want?" She smiled. The real smile. The one I'd fallen in love with. "I want you to stay," she said. "I want you to love me. I want you to watch me. I want you to choose me, even knowing what I am. That's what she wanted. That's what she was afraid of. She was afraid you'd leave." "I won't." "I know." Her thumb traced my cheekbone. "I know you won't." That night, I tried to leave. Not because I wanted to. Because I needed to prove something—to myself, to her, I don't know. I went to the front door. I put my hand on the knob. I turned. It didn't open. I tried again. I tried the deadbolt, the chain, the locks I'd installed myself two years ago after the neighbor's break-in. Nothing. The door didn't move. I went to the window. I tried to raise it—stuck, the old wood swollen in the frame, I'd been meaning to fix it for months. I pulled harder. Nothing. I looked at her. She was standing in the hallway, watching me. Arms folded. Head tilted. The same position she'd been in when Father Delgado was here. The same position she'd been in for weeks. "I told you," she said. "I need you to stay." "Let me go." "I can't." She smiled. "You can leave whenever you want. The door will open. The window will open. You can walk right out." I went back to the door. I tried the knob again. It opened. I stood there, in the doorway, one foot in the hallway, one foot in the apartment. I could leave. I could walk away. I could go to a hotel, call someone, get help. I didn't move. She was still smiling. "I know," she said. "I knew you wouldn't leave. She knew too. That's why she wrote the letter. Because she knew you'd stay. Because she knew you'd watch. Because that's what you do, M. That's what you've always done." I closed the door. I stood there, my back against the wood, my breath coming hard. And I understood, finally, that she'd been letting me try. The calls to Father Delgado, the attempts to leave, the offers to let me go. She'd been letting me make those calls because it didn't matter. Because I was never going to leave. Because I'd been watching her for years before any of this started, and watching was the same as staying, and staying was the same as loving, and she knew it. She'd always known it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a significant escalation in the possession horror story, effectively building tension while maintaining psychological complexity. The writing demonstrates strong craft in several key areas. The characterization is particularly well-executed. The narrator Michael's obsessive, analytical nature comes through clearly in his voice - the detail about marking days in his notebook, his need to catalog and understand everything. Eleanor/the entity is genuinely unsettling, particularly in how it weaponizes intimate knowledge against Father Delgado and Michael. The revelation of deeply personal secrets creates genuine horror through violation of privacy and trust rather than cheap scares. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and building dread. The entity's speech patterns effectively convey something wearing Eleanor like a mask, especially in lines like "Talk. That's what you want to do? Talk?" The way it shifts between Eleanor's voice and something alien is well-handled. The chapter follows its planned structure faithfully, bringing Father Delgado back with help, showing the entity's true power, and ending with Michael's realization about his own complicity. The door sequence is particularly effective - the entity doesn't need to trap him because it understands he won't leave. The horror works on multiple levels: supernatural (the entity's knowledge and power), psychological (Michael's recognition of his own voyeuristic nature), and relational (the corruption of intimate knowledge). The ending revelation that Michael has been "watching" Eleanor long before the possession adds disturbing depth to his character. However, there are some weaknesses. Sister Catherine feels underdeveloped - more of a plot device than a character. Some of the entity's omniscient revelations about Father Delgado, while effective for horror, strain credibility slightly. The pacing occasionally slows during exposition. The prose is generally clean and effective, avoiding purple language while maintaining atmospheric tension. The writing shows good control of sentence rhythm and paragraph structure to build suspense. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 19 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7: The Choice I stopped trying to leave on a Thursday. I don't know why Thursday. Maybe because the radiator clicked louder on Thursdays. Maybe because the light through the window was a particular shade of gray. Maybe because I'd finally run out of reasons to pretend. I sat on the couch. She sat next to me. The space between us was exactly twelve inches—the same space there had always been, the distance of two people who loved each other and had learned how to share silence. "Tell me," I said. "Tell me what you are." She was quiet for a long time. Not the false quiet, the performed stillness. The real kind—the kind that happens when something is choosing what to say. "I'm not sure," she said finally. "I don't think I have a name. I don't think there are words for what I am. But I can tell you what I want." "What do you want?" "I want to understand." She turned to look at me. Her eyes were different today—not flat, not still, but something else. Something almost sad. "I've been watching you watch her. For weeks now. For months, maybe. Before you knew I was here. I watched the way you watched her, and I didn't understand." "Understand what?" "Love." She said the word like she was testing it. "I don't have a word for what you feel. I can see it—I've seen it, in her memories, in the way she thought about you. But I don't understand it. I don't understand why you stay. I don't understand why you watch. I don't understand why you keep making lists instead of leaving." "I told you—" "I know." She reached out and took my hand. Her fingers interlaced with mine. It felt like Eleanor's hand—same calluses, same warmth, same way her thumb rested against my knuckle. "I know you love her. That's what I want to understand. That's what I want to *be*." "Be?" "I want to know what it's like." Her voice was quiet. "To be loved. To be watched. To have someone choose to stay, even when it's hard. That's what I want, Michael. That's why I'm here. Not to hurt you. Not to take anything. Just to understand." "You're using her." "I'm wearing her. I'm carrying her. She's in here somewhere—I can feel her, sometimes, like a voice in another room. She screams sometimes. She cries. But she's quiet now. She's so tired." I wanted to pull away. I wanted to scream at her, hit her, shake her until Eleanor came back. But I didn't. I sat there, her hand in mine, twelve inches of silence between us. "You could stay," she said. "You could love this. It would be easier." "Easier than what?" "Than fighting. Than calling priests. Than pretending you can save her." She leaned closer. Her head rested on my shoulder—her hair smelled like her shampoo, the same shampoo, the same Eleanor, the same everything. "She knew you couldn't save her. That's why she wrote the letter. She knew you'd stay anyway. She knew you'd choose her, even like this. Even knowing what I am." "I haven't chosen anything." "You've chosen everything." Her breath was warm against my neck. "Every day you stay. Every night you watch. Every time you pretend to sleep instead of leaving. That's a choice, M. That's love. That's what I want to understand." I sat there for a long time. Then I found my phone. Father Delgado's number was still saved—I'd added it after the first call, just in case. I typed out a message: *Please come back. I need help. I need more people. I need it to stop.* I pressed send. She didn't move. She didn't try to stop me. She just sat there, her head on my shoulder, her hand in mine, watching me with Eleanor's eyes. "You called them," she said. "I have to." "I know." She smiled. "I knew you would. That's okay." "Why is it okay?" "Because it doesn't matter." She sat up, looked at me, and for a moment—just a moment—I saw something behind her eyes. Something that wasn't her. Something that was old and vast and patient. "You can call everyone. You can bring all the priests, all the holy water, all the words in all the languages. It won't change anything. I'm not going anywhere." "We'll find a way." "Maybe." She reached up and touched my face. Her fingers traced my jaw, my cheekbone, the stubble I hadn't shaved in days. "But here's the thing, M. Here's what I need you to understand." She leaned in close. Her lips were next to my ear. "When they try to push me out," she whispered, "I'll take you with me. That's the deal. That's what I want. Not your body. Not your soul. Just your watching. Just your staying. Just the way you love, Michael. That's what I want. That's what I'll keep." Her breath was warm. Her voice was soft. And in that voice—in that whisper—I heard something I recognized. A memory. The first night we'd spent together, in this apartment, twelve years ago, before we were married, before we knew anything. The way she'd whispered in the dark: *Stay. Stay with me. Stay.* I felt my chest break. "That's not her," I said. "That's not Eleanor." "It's everything she was," she said. "It's everything she'll ever be. And now it's mine." The phone buzzed. Father Delgado: *We'll be there tomorrow. Get out while you can.* I looked at the message. I looked at her. She smiled. "Tomorrow," she said. "We have tonight." She took my hand again. She leaned her head on my shoulder. And I stayed.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a significant turning point in the possession horror story, where the narrator Michael finally confronts the entity inhabiting his wife Eleanor. The writing demonstrates strong adherence to both the established character profiles and the chapter plan, delivering a psychologically complex scene that maintains the story's central tension. The characterization is particularly strong. Michael's voice remains consistent with his established profile as an obsessive watcher who rationalizes everything. His internal monologue captures his analytical nature ("The space between us was exactly twelve inches") while revealing his emotional vulnerability. The entity wearing Eleanor is well-crafted, speaking with an alien curiosity about human love while maintaining Eleanor's physical mannerisms. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and building dread. The chapter excels at showing rather than telling. Rather than simply stating that Michael is torn between love and horror, we see it in his actions: sitting exactly twelve inches away, holding her hand despite knowing what she is, making the call to Father Delgado while she watches. The entity's nature is revealed through specific details - her different eyes, her clinical curiosity about love, her patient acceptance of his call for help. The horror builds effectively through psychological tension rather than gore or jump scares. The entity's whispered threat ("I'll take you with me") and her use of Eleanor's intimate memory creates genuine dread. The inversion of the original loving whisper ("Stay with me") into something sinister is particularly effective. The writing maintains coherence with previous chapters while advancing the plot meaningfully. Michael's decision to stop trying to leave feels earned given his established character and the mounting evidence of the entity's power. The chapter sets up the final confrontation while deepening our understanding of what's at stake. However, there are some minor weaknesses. Occasionally the prose veers toward the overly literary ("I felt my chest break") without quite earning the emotional weight. Some metaphorical language feels slightly forced, though not egregiously so. The pacing is generally strong but could benefit from more variation in sentence structure in places. The chapter successfully follows its outlined plan, delivering the promised confrontation where the entity explains its motives and Michael makes his final call for help. It maintains the story's central themes about love, watching, and the horror of losing someone while they're still physically present. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8: After Father Delgado came at dawn. There were three of them this time—him, Sister Catherine, and a man I'd never seen, older, heavy-set, with hands like shovels and a face like he'd stopped being surprised by anything a long time ago. He didn't introduce himself. He didn't need to. They brought a bag. I didn't ask what was in it. "She's in the bedroom," I said. "She's been sleeping." "Has she?" Father Delgado looked at me. His eyes were red-rimmed, tired. He'd been doing this a long time. I could see it in the way he moved—like someone who'd stopped expecting things to get better. They went in without knocking. I stayed in the doorway. She was sitting up in bed. Awake. Waiting. "Hello again," she said. The man with the shovel hands didn't say anything. He just opened the bag and started laying things out on the dresser—bottles, candles, a book with no title on the cover, something that looked like a rope with knots tied at intervals. "We don't have to do this," Father Delgado said. "We can talk. We can—" "You can do what you came to do." She smiled. "Or you can leave. I'm not going to stop you." "You're not going to stop us." "No." Her eyes moved to me, standing in the doorway. "He's not going to stop you either. He's already made his choice." The man with the shovel hands started praying. Not in Latin—something older, something that felt like it had weight, like the words themselves were pressing down on the air. Sister Catherine opened the bottles. Father Delgado took out a cross, wooden, simple. Eleanor screamed. It wasn't her voice. It was never her voice anymore. It was a sound that filled the room, that pressed against my chest, that made my teeth ache. She writhed on the bed—not like a person in pain, but like something being pulled, stretched, torn. Her arms flailed. Her back arched. Her mouth opened and closed and opened again, and different voices came out—hers, his, theirs, something that sounded like static, like radio tuning between stations. Father Delgado sprinkled water. The screaming got louder. "You can't do this," Eleanor said—or the thing inside her said, in a voice that was half hers and half something else. "I'm not going anywhere. I told you. I told him. I'm not—" " in nomine Patris, et Filii, et Spiritus Sancti—" She flew backward. Not metaphorically—physically, her body hitting the headboard with a crack that made me flinch. The candles flickered. The lights went out. In the darkness, I could see her face, and it wasn't her face—it was something else, something that wore her features like a mask that didn't quite fit, something with too much hatred in its eyes. "Stop," she said. And then, in a different voice: "Please." The man with the shovel hands kept praying. Sister Catherine was crying—silent tears, steady, like she'd been through this before. "Michael." Eleanor's voice. The real one. Desperate. "Michael, please. Remember what I whispered. Remember." I didn't know what she meant. I didn't want to know what she meant. "Michael—" "Keep going," Father Delgado said. The prayer got louder. The water hit her like acid. And then— Silence. She lay on the bed, still. Her chest was rising and falling. Her eyes were closed. Father Delgado lowered the cross. Sister Catherine wiped her face. The man with the shovel hands put the candles out, one by one. "It's done," he said. I stepped forward. I touched her hand. It was warm. It was hers. "Eleanor." Her eyes opened. Brown. Human. Present. "Michael?" Her voice was a rasp, a whisper, the voice of someone who'd been asleep for a long time. "What—what happened?" "You've been sick," I said. The lie came out before I could stop it. "You've been sleeping for a while." "Sleeping?" She looked around the room—at the candles, at the men, at me. "What's—why are you crying?" I touched my face. My fingers were wet. "I don't know," I said. She sat up slowly. She looked at Father Delgado, at Sister Catherine, at the man with the shovel hands. She frowned. "Do I know you?" she asked Father Delgado. "No," he said. "We've never met." "Then why—" She stopped. She looked at me again. "Michael, what's wrong?" I didn't answer. I couldn't answer. She remembered everything. That was the first thing I tested—the trip to Portland, the book in the diner, the way she used to hum while making coffee. She remembered all of it. She remembered my mother's name. She remembered our wedding. She remembered the first night, in this apartment, in the dark, the way she'd whispered: *Stay.* But she didn't remember the letter. She didn't remember staring at me in the dark. She didn't remember saying *you're scared of me* or *you should have left* or *when they try to push me out, I'll take you with me.* She was Eleanor. She was my wife. She was the woman I'd loved for twelve years. But something was different. Something was wrong. I couldn't name it. I couldn't point to it. But when she smiled at me, I waited for the half-second delay. When she touched my face, I waited for the cold. When she said *I love you*, I waited for the echo of something older. She didn't notice. She didn't remember. She was fine. That night, she fell asleep beside me. I lay there, watching her breathe. The covers were pulled up to her chin. Her hair was spread on the pillow. She was breathing the way she used to—shallow, little sighs, the sounds I fell asleep to for years. She was back. She was fine. I couldn't close my eyes. I lay there, watching her, and I thought about what she'd whispered. *When they try to push me out, I'll take you with me.* I thought about the half-second delay, the flat eyes, the wrong smile. I thought about the scratches on the wall that I'd never found again, the letter that had disappeared from my desk, the thing that had worn my wife like a coat. I thought about how I was still watching. How I'd always been watching. How I'd never stopped. She stirred. Her eyes opened—just barely, just a slit, just enough to see. "Go to sleep, M," she whispered. And for a moment—just a moment—I thought I saw something behind her eyes. Something flat. Something still. Something waiting. Then she closed her eyes. She turned away. She went back to breathing. I lay there. I didn't sleep. I didn't close my eyes. I watched her all night, and I thought about what love was, in the end. Watching. Staying. Pretending you don't notice when something behind your wife's eyes doesn't blink quite right. Pretending you don't hear her whisper in the dark. Pretending she's still Eleanor. And I understood, finally, that it didn't matter whether she'd been saved. It didn't matter whether the thing was gone. I was the one who was caught now—trapped in the watching, in the waiting, in the fear that someday soon, she'd start staring again. And I'd pretend to sleep. Because that's what love is. That's what it always was.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter serves as the climactic resolution to a possession horror story, and it largely succeeds in delivering a satisfying yet unsettling conclusion. The writing demonstrates strong technical control and psychological insight, though it occasionally stumbles in execution. The chapter's greatest strength lies in its psychological authenticity. Michael's obsessive watching behavior feels genuinely disturbing rather than merely plot-convenient, and the ambiguous ending brilliantly captures the paranoia that would realistically follow such an experience. The narrator's voice remains consistent throughout - his tendency to rationalize, his attention to physical details, and his compulsive need to catalog and control all ring true to the established character. The exorcism scene itself is handled with restraint and effectiveness. Rather than relying on over-the-top supernatural theatrics, the author focuses on specific, visceral details: Eleanor's body hitting the headboard, the different voices emerging, the candles flickering. The dialogue during this sequence feels natural and urgent, particularly Father Delgado's weary professionalism and Eleanor's desperate pleas. The story's central horror concept - that love itself might be a form of surveillance - is sophisticated and well-executed. The final paragraphs effectively invert the opening premise, with Michael now the one being watched while pretending to sleep, creating a satisfying structural echo while deepening the psychological horror. However, the chapter suffers from some pacing issues. The transition from the exorcism to Eleanor's recovery feels somewhat rushed, and certain moments lack the specificity that characterizes the story's best passages. The repeated emphasis on "watching" occasionally becomes heavy-handed, though it generally serves the thematic purpose. The ambiguous ending works well thematically but could have been more subtly executed. The line "That's what love is. That's what it always was" feels slightly overwrought, pushing the theme too explicitly rather than trusting the reader to understand the implications. The chapter successfully follows its outlined plan and maintains fidelity to both the writing prompt and established character profiles. Michael's voice remains consistent, and the resolution feels earned rather than convenient. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a sophisticated psychological horror story that demonstrates strong technical craft and genuine literary merit. The piece successfully builds tension through subtle character observation and psychological deterioration rather than relying on cheap scares or gore. The narrative structure is well-executed, with each chapter escalating the horror while maintaining internal consistency. The first-person narrator Michael is believable as someone caught between love and terror, documenting his wife's transformation with obsessive detail that feels authentic rather than contrived. His voice remains consistent throughout - analytical, desperate, and ultimately tragic. The characterization of Eleanor/the entity is particularly nuanced. Rather than a simple possession story, this explores the blurred lines between identity, memory, and consciousness. The entity's gradual revelation that it doesn't just inhabit Eleanor but has become her creates genuine philosophical horror. The detail about the entity learning love through watching Michael watch Eleanor is both disturbing and oddly touching. The pacing is expertly controlled, building from subtle wrongness (stopped humming, perfect dishwasher loading) to overt supernatural elements, then pulling back to ambiguity in the final chapter. Each revelation feels earned rather than arbitrary. The world-building is grounded and realistic - the Minneapolis setting, the specific apartment details, the mundane relationship dynamics all create a believable foundation that makes the supernatural elements more unsettling. The dialogue serves the story well, particularly the entity's increasingly confident voice and the heartbreaking moments when Eleanor's real voice breaks through. The conversations with Father Delgado feel authentic to the situation. The story avoids purple prose while maintaining literary quality. The metaphors (butter scraped over bread, watching as love) are effective and consistent with the narrator's analytical nature. The ending is genuinely ambiguous - we're left uncertain whether Eleanor is truly saved or if Michael is now trapped in perpetual paranoid watching. Some minor weaknesses: occasional repetitive phrasing around Michael's obsessive cataloging, and the religious intervention sequence feels slightly rushed compared to the careful buildup. The scratches on the wall detail disappears without explanation, which may be intentional ambiguity but feels unresolved. Overall, this is a mature, well-crafted horror story that earns its emotional impact through character development and psychological insight rather than shock value. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 18 Nuanced Characters: 19 Pacing: 17 World Building: 16 Compelling Plot: 18 Emotionally Engaging: 19 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 2 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Unearned Transformations: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 20
Avg Final Item Score: 87.1/100
+ Pulp Revenge Tale — Babysitter's Payback (Chapter Avg: 75.8 | Final: 61.3)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Pulp Revenge Tale Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: They fucked with the wrong babysitter. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Writing Plan: "They Fucked with the Wrong Babysitter" --- # Brainstorming ## Character Concepts **The Babysitter (Protagonist)** - **Option A: The Hidden Warrior** – Former military operative (Marine, CIA, special forces) who left that life behind but never truly left. Now she babysits to live a "normal" life, but her past catches up when someone targets the family she's protecting. - **Option B: The Avenging Mother Figure** – A woman who lost a child or was assaulted while babysitting years ago. She now specifically seeks out situations where children are in danger to protect them—and destroys anyone who threatens her charges. - **Option C: The Forensic Nightmare** – A brilliant hacker/detective who goes undercover as a babysitter to investigate wealthy families suspected of abuse. When she discovers the truth, she doesn't go to the cops—she becomes the judge, jury, and executioner. - **Option D: The Final Girl** – A survivor of a mass murder who was the only one who escaped. She became a babysitter to be around children—the innocence she lost—and when the killers return or someone new threatens kids in her orbit, she finishes what they started. - **Option E: The Immovable Object** – A seemingly sweet, unassuming young woman who is actually a sociopath who channels her violence through protecting children. Her love for her charges is genuine, but her methods are terrifying. **The Antagonists ("They")** - **Option A: The Cartel** – Drug runners who use the house she's working at as a drop point. When she witnesses something she shouldn't, they come for her—and she annihilates them. - **Option B: The Powerful Family** – Wealthy parents who abuse their children. She discovers the truth and they try to silence her. - **Option C: The Serial Killers** – A pair or group of predators who target babysitters. She was their intended victim once; now she's the hunter. - **Option D: The Corrupt Elite** – A network of powerful men who prey on young women and children. They made the mistake of threatening someone she loves. - **Option E: The Ex** – An abusive ex-boyfriend who tracks her down to the job, expecting her to be the vulnerable woman she was. She is not that woman anymore. **Supporting Characters** - The Child: Could be young (5-8), older (10-12), or a teenager. The child's relationship to the protagonist can be purely professional or evolve into something deeper—like a surrogate daughter/son. - The Other Babysitter: A friend or colleague who gets pulled into the chaos, providing someone to talk to and grounds the story in reality. - The Father/Mother: Could be an ally who doesn't know what she's capable of, or an antagonist themselves. - The Detective: A cop who follows the trail of bodies she leaves, serving as both threat and reluctant ally. ## Plot Directions **Version 1: The Night It Happened** The story unfolds in real-time over one night. She takes a babysitting job at an isolated mansion. The parents are away. Something is wrong. The house is invaded. She defends the children within. Then she hunts down the invaders' masters. Chapters 1-3 cover the invasion and survival, chapters 4-6 the hunt, chapters 7-8 the final confrontation. **Version 2: The Backstory Unfolds** We open with her in the aftermath—bodies around her, police arriving. The story then unfolds in flashback to show who wronged her and why, intercut with the present-day consequences. Her true history is revealed piecemeal. **Version 3: The Reckoning** She learns that "they" have hurt other babysitters, other children. She goes after the organization systematically. Each chapter is a different target, building to the leader. More episodic structure. **Version 4: The Frame** She is framed for a crime she didn't commit (or did). She has to clear her name while evading both police and the real perpetrators, getting revenge in the process. ## Tone & Genre Conventions - **Hardboiled Pulp**: Spare prose, vivid violence, world-weary protagonist with a code, snappy dialogue, morally simple world where bad people need killing - **Feminist Revenge**: Subverts the "final girl" trope—the babysitter isn't victimized; she's the threat. Think *Pepper's Ghost* or *The Last House on the Left* energy - **Grindhouse B-Movie**: Excessive, gleeful violence, pulpy dialogue, exploitation elements played straight, stylized action - **Neo-Noir**: Modern setting, morally gray, rain and shadows, noir sensibility applied to contemporary world - **Supernatural Undertones**: Could add a slight edge of the uncanny—she's almost too good, too relentless, too unknowable ## Story Beats (Eight-Chapter Structure) - **Ch 1**: Establish normal world—she's a babysitter, she's good at it, we see her with the kids. Something is off. The parents are late. Phone lines go dead. Cars approach. - **Ch 2**: The threat arrives. The invasion begins. She reveals capabilities under pressure. First kills. The night turns to survival mode. - **Ch 3**: Morning comes. She has survived. She discovers who they were, why they came. Perhaps she finds evidence of others they've hurt. The decision is made—she's not just going to survive, she's going to destroy them. - **Ch 4**: She goes on the offensive. First targets. Investigation, tracking, preparation. We learn more about her past. Police interest begins. - **Ch 5**: Midpoint reversal—she encounters a setback. Someone she trusted. A fight goes wrong. She's wounded. The enemies know she's coming. - **Ch 6**: She regroups, adapts. The final plan forms. Meanwhile, the main antagonist makes their move—more targets, more stakes. - **Ch 7**: The assault on the enemy's headquarters/lair. All-out action. Allies may be lost. She fights through. - **Ch 8**: Final confrontation with the leader. Resolution. She walks away. Perhaps we see her with another charge, another child—the cycle continues, or peace at last. ## Pacing Considerations - Front-load action in chapters 1-2 to hook the reader - Slow down in chapters 3-4 to build mystery and character - Chapter 5 is the crisis—everything goes wrong - Chapters 6-8 escalate continuously to the climax - Each chapter should end on a hook or reversal --- # Reflection ## What Stands Out The prompt "They fucked with the wrong babysitter" carries immediate power because it inverts expectations. Babysitters are culturally coded as vulnerable—young women alone in strangers' homes, responsible for children. The phrase promises that this assumption will be shattered. There's also a secondary inversion: "they" suggests a group, implying the protagonist will take on multiple enemies, multiple enemies who are probably powerful and dangerous. The revenge angle gives us a protagonist with clear motivation and a moral simplicity that works well for pulp. We don't need to question whether she's justified; the prompt tells us "they" did something wrong. This frees us to focus on the execution—the how and the journey, not the whether. Eight chapters at ~1000 words each gives us 8000 words, which is substantial enough for character development and a complete arc but tight enough to maintain momentum. This is a sprint, not a marathon—we don't have room for meandering subplots. ## What Needs Work The biggest risk is making the protagonist a "cool girl" fantasy—too capable, too invulnerable, too perfect. We need enough vulnerability or humanity to care about her. She should be scary in her competence, but there should be a reason she's this way. The audience needs to understand her, even if they don't fully know her history. The antagonists need to be genuinely threatening. If "they" are just incompetent goons, the revenge lacks tension. We need to see or hear about what they're capable of—make the stakes real. The babysitter-child relationship is crucial. If we don't care about the kid(s), the protagonist's revenge feels less earned. The child can be a plot device, but they'd be better as a genuine character. Balancing the pulp tone with the subject matter (children potentially in danger) requires care. The violence should be pulp-excessive but not gratuitously traumatic. We're not writing horror; we're writing revenge fantasy. The audience should feel satisfied, not disturbed. The "they" needs to be specific enough to be believable but not so specific that it limits the story. "They" could be a criminal organization, a powerful family, a network—something that allows for multiple targets and escalating stakes. ## Potential Approaches to Consider A historical setting (1970s?) would lean into the pulp aesthetic and allow for period-appropriate attitudes and technology, but it also creates distance from the reader. A contemporary setting grounds it but requires handling modern tech (phones, cameras, social media) deliberately—either using it or accounting for why it doesn't matter. A frame narrative (past action + present consequences) could work but might slow down the revenge momentum. A tight timeline (one night, or 24 hours) creates urgency but limits scope for the revenge portion. The most resonant version will likely be one where the babysitter's past is revealed gradually—we know she's dangerous and we know she's justified, but the specifics emerge as the story unfolds. This creates both mystery and payoff. --- This brainstorming and reflection should provide a solid foundation for developing a specific plan and eventually writing the story. The core strengths are the inversion of expectations (vulnerable becomes dangerous), the moral clarity of revenge, and the pulp tone that allows for stylized action and dialogue. The key challenges are maintaining character depth within genre constraints, making the antagonists genuinely threatening, and balancing the subject matter with the desired tone.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Formulated Intentions for "They Fucked with the Wrong Babysitter"** This story is a hardboiled pulp revenge tale that inverts the vulnerable babysitter trope by making her the most dangerous person in any room. The tone will be lean, atmospheric, and violent without being gratuitous—excessive in the way B-movie pulp operates, but grounded enough in character that the violence carries emotional weight. **Plot Choice:** The story will follow a tight timeline over approximately 72 hours. The protagonist takes a routine babysitting job at an isolated estate. When armed men come for the family's secrets, she kills them all—but not before learning they're part of a criminal network that preys on babysitters and the children in their care. This isn't just about one night; this is about a pattern. She decides to dismantle the entire organization. **Protagonist:** She is a former Marine (or similar elite background) who left that life behind but never fully left it. She babysits because she needs something normal, something innocent. She's skilled, controlled, and terrifyingly competent—but she has a line she won't cross and a softness she protects fiercely. Her past is implied, not explained in detail; the reader learns what they need to know through her actions. **Antagonists:** A regional criminal network that uses wealthy families as fronts and views babysitters as disposable—either as witnesses to eliminate or, worse, as part of their operation. They have money, men, and reach. They are genuinely dangerous. The "they" in the prompt is this organization, led by a figure who never appears until the end. **The Child:** A seven-year-old girl, Emma, left in the protagonist's care. She witnesses enough to understand that her babysitter is not what she appears—and this doesn't scare her. It comforts her. The protagonist's protectiveness toward Emma is the emotional engine of the story; her revenge is partly for Emma, partly for all the others. **Tone & Style:** Third-person limited, following the protagonist. Prose will be punchy and direct—short sentences, vivid imagery, minimal interiority. The violence is impactful because it's rare; she doesn't enjoy killing, but she's very good at it. Dialogue is sparse and sharp. The pulp aesthetic will be supported by period-appropriate contemporary realism—no technology that would complicate the story unnecessarily. **What I'm Aiming For:** - A protagonist who is frightening in her competence but sympathetic in her motivation - Rising tension that compounds with each chapter - A satisfying revenge arc that feels earned, not easy - Pulp satisfaction—style, violence, cool moments—without sacrificing character - A resonant ending that doesn't over-explain **What I'm Avoiding:** - Making her a "cool girl" fantasy—too perfect, too invincible - Random or motiveless violence for shock value - Antagonists who are cartoonishly stupid - Excessive backstory dumps - The child becoming merely a plot device --- # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: The Job The protagonist (I'll use the name Mara for now) arrives at the Whitmore estate for a weekend babysitting job. She's done this dozens of times—wealthy family, distant parents, sweet kid. We see her with Emma, the seven-year-old, and their easy rapport. The parents are rushed, distracted—they're expecting guests that never arrive. Mara notices small wrongnesses: the nervous energy of the staff, the cars that pass too slowly on the road, the phone line that seems to have interference. The parents leave. It's just Mara and Emma now. Mara puts Emma to bed. She waits. *Purpose: Establish character, relationship, atmosphere, and the first hints that something is off.* ## Chapter 2: The Night The intruders come at 2 AM. Three men, professional, armed. They expect no resistance—they expect a terrified young woman and an easy entry. They are wrong. Mara dispatches them with brutal efficiency, using the house's layout, improvised weapons, and skills that hint at a violent past. Emma witnesses the aftermath from the stairs—blood, bodies, her babysitter standing in the wreckage, phone in hand, calling 911. The police arrive. The story spreads. Mara gives her statement. But she's not leaving. She's found something on one of the men—something that connects to a pattern. *Purpose: The inversion. The violence. The reveal of who Mara really is—not explained, just shown.* ## Chapter 3: The Pattern Mara stays in town, telling the Whitmores she's concerned for Emma's safety. In reality, she's digging. Using old contacts and skills, she discovers that the men who attacked were connected to a larger organization—a network that operates across the region, using wealthy families as fronts and babysitters as unwitting participants or targets. She finds reports, missing persons, cases that were never solved. Other girls. Other children. This wasn't random. This was business. Mara makes a decision: she's not just going to survive this. She's going to end it. *Purpose: Expand the scope. Justify the revenge. Show Mara's capability beyond combat.* ## Chapter 4: The Hunt Mara begins systematically dismantling the network's outer operations. She identifies a mid-level operator—a man who manages the logistics, who moves the product, who sleeps with guns under his pillow. She takes him in a confrontation that leaves no witnesses. Then another. Each kill is deliberate, surgical, and sends a message: someone is coming for you. But the network notices. A power player named Carver begins to investigate this "babysitter" who's making waves. The hunt is on in both directions. *Purpose: Escalation. Show Mara's investigation and combat skills in tandem. Introduce the main antagonist's attention.* ## Chapter 5: The Setback Mara's next target knows she's coming. It's a trap. She's ambushed in an abandoned warehouse, outnumbered, wounded. She escapes through sheer will and skill, but she's hurt—physically and emotionally. Worse, she's made a mistake that cost her time. Carver has figured out who she is, where she came from. He's also figured out her weakness: Emma. He makes a move. Mara receives a message: stop, or the girl dies. *Purpose: The midpoint crisis. Everything goes wrong. Stakes raised to the personal.* ## Chapter 6: The Reckoning Mara refuses to stop. She knows Carver won't keep his word—these people never do. Instead, she goes on the offensive, using her knowledge of his operation to hit him where it hurts: his money, his reputation, his people. She recruits an unlikely ally—a cop who's seen the corruption this network enables and is willing to look the other way. The plan comes together: a final assault on Carver's primary location, his private compound where he keeps his darkest secrets. It's do or die. *Purpose: Rebound. The new plan. Gathering forces for the climax.* ## Chapter 7: The Assault Mara leads a direct assault on Carver's compound. It's violent, relentless, and deeply personal. She fights through guards, security systems, and Carver's best men. Bodies pile up. She finds evidence of what the network truly does—the rooms, the records, the proof. Emma is not here, but other children were. The rage fuels her. Finally, she reaches Carver's inner sanctum. He's waiting. He's not defenseless. *Purpose: The action climax. Maximum intensity. The culmination of everything she's built toward.* ## Chapter 8: The Morning Mara kills Carver. It's not clean, not easy, but it's done. She stands in the wreckage of his operation, covered in evidence of his guilt—and her own. The police arrive—not her ally, but the full force of the law. She has minutes to disappear. She does, leaving behind enough evidence to bring down what remains of the network. Epilogue: Some time later. Mara is in a different city, a different house, a different child's bedroom. She reads a newspaper report on the Carver case—the arrests, the convictions, the end of the network. Emma is safe. Other children will be safe. The cycle continues. She puts down the paper and goes to check on her new charge. *Purpose: Resolution. Moral ambiguity. The understanding that this is who she is now—not a survivor, but a guardian. The cycle continues.*
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# Critique & Brainstorming: Making It Human ## How a Human Might Approach This Particular Piece A human writer coming to "They fucked with the wrong babysitter" would likely start from a much more specific, personal place. They'd have a reason they want to write this story—maybe they were a babysitter themselves as a teenager and remember the specific vulnerability of those hours in a stranger's home. Maybe they read about a case that made them furious. Maybe they just think it's goddamn unjust that young women are told to be careful, to carry keys between their fingers, to fear the night—and they want to write a story where that fear gets answered with extreme prejudice. A human wouldn't approach it as a "pulp revenge tale" with "intentions" and "chapter purposes." They'd feel their way into it. They'd know what excites them about the premise: maybe the inversion (the final girl who isn't a final girl, who's the threat), maybe the isolation (that house, those hours, the world narrowed to one woman and her will), maybe the particular violence of it. They'd write toward that excitement. Humans also make weird choices. A human might write the babysitter as a man, which completely reframes the gendered vulnerability. A human might make the kid older, a teenager who complicates things. A human might set it in a specific year they remember, with specific music, specific anxieties. A human might hate endings and leave it ambiguous. A human might love revenge narratives and lean all the way into the gleeful destruction. A human might be very, very angry about something in the culture and write that anger into every page. The key is: a human writer has obsessions, specificities, idiosyncratic reasons for telling any particular story. They don't optimize for "what works"—they write from a place of genuine (even if unnamed) need. --- ## Telltale LLM Approaches to Writing (Generally) LLMs have been trained on enormous corpora of "good" writing, which means they tend to produce writing that is: **Safe and Risk-Averse** - Avoids controversy, anything that might offend or discomfort - Stays within established genre conventions without subverting them - Balances everything to the point of blandness - Doesn't push against the reader's expectations (or does so predictably) **Over-Structured** - Every chapter has a clear "purpose" (as in my plan) - Three-act structure is followed almost mathematically - Setup, payoff, escalation—everything is mapped - No room for the messy, the unplanned, the structural accidents that make human writing alive **Explanatory** - Tells the reader what they should feel or understand - Over-explains motivation - "She was terrifyingly competent" instead of showing us terrifying competence - Wraps up everything with a bow—ambiguity makes the model uncomfortable **Plausible to a Fault** - Protagonists are capable but never truly frightening - Antagonists are functional but rarely genuinely menacing - Violence is implied rather than rendered - The world is more coherent than reality, less chaotic **Hedging and Qualifying** - "Perhaps," "in some ways," "somewhat"—weakening language that undermines conviction - "She seemed" when "she was" would be stronger - Avoiding direct statements in favor of cautious approximations **Trying to Impress** - Elevated vocabulary where plain language would do - Stylized prose that draws attention to itself - "Beautiful" sentences that aren't doing anything functional - The sound of a thesaurus, not a voice **Tonal Uniformity** - Same register throughout—professional "good" prose - No shifts from sparse to lush, from controlled to raw - Everything at the same temperature, the same "quality" **Agreeable** - Doesn't challenge the reader - Doesn't make them uncomfortable - Says what it thinks the reader wants to hear - Avoids the genuinely transgressive or disturbing --- ## Deep Dive Critique: The Intention & Plan Now let me apply this critique specifically to what I wrote. ### The Protagonist (Mara) I described her as: "a former Marine (or similar elite background) who left that life behind but never fully left it. She babysits because she needs something normal, something innocent. She's skilled, controlled, and terrifyingly competent—but she has a line she won't cross and a softness she protects fiercely." **LLM Tropes Here:** - "Former Marine with a past" is the most common "dangerous woman" character in genre fiction. It's safe. It explains everything without requiring anything specific. - The balance—"terrifyingly competent BUT has a softness"—is exactly the kind of hedging that makes characters feel engineered. She's competent AND soft. Tough BUT tender. It's designed to make her likable. - "She has a line she won't cross" is explaining her moral code explicitly rather than showing us where her lines are through her actions. **How to Make It More Human:** - Make her more frightening. Not "terrifyingly competent" in a controlled way—make her actually scary, maybe to the reader too. Maybe she's not fully in control. Maybe her violence is not clean. - Give her a specific reason for babysitting that's not generic "she wants something normal." Maybe she lost a child. Maybe she was a child in a bad situation and this is her reclamation of those hours. Get specific. - Maybe she's not Marine—maybe she's something else, something less tropey. Maybe she was a cop who got cashiered for excessive force. Maybe she's never been in the military at all; she just learned to fight because she had to. ### The Antagonists ("They") "A regional criminal network that uses wealthy families as fronts and views babysitters as disposable." **LLM Tropes Here:** - This is vague. It's a "network," a "criminal organization." It's functional but has no texture. - The vagueness is safe—it doesn't commit to anything specific, which means it can't be wrong. - "Views babysitters as disposable" is explaining their motivation rather than showing it. **How to Make It More Human:** - Make them specific. Name them. Give them a history. Maybe they're not a "network" at all—they're one family, one man, something grounded. - Make them more chilling in their specificity. Not "they're criminals," but "this is what they do, this is how they operate, this particular thing they do is what makes Mara lose control." - Maybe one of them isn't a stereotype—maybe one of the attackers is young, scared, didn't want to be there. Maybe she has to kill someone who begs. ### The Child (Emma) "A seven-year-old girl, Emma, left in the protagonist's care. She witnesses enough to understand that her babysitter is not what she appears—and this doesn't scare her. It comforts her." **LLM Tropes Here:** - She's too perfect. She witnesses violence and... she's comforted? That's convenient for the emotional engine. - She's functional—"the emotional engine of the story." She's there to give Mara motivation. - The relationship is too neat: Mara protects Emma, Emma provides emotional stakes. **How to Make It More Human:** - Let Emma be more complicated. Maybe she's traumatized by what she sees. Maybe she asks questions that Mara can't answer. Maybe she's not comforted—she's changed. - Give Emma agency. Maybe she does something that matters, something that affects the plot, not just Mara's motivation. - Maybe Emma isn't a perfect angel kid—maybe she's difficult, angry,acting out. How does Mara protect someone who isn't easy to love? ### The Violence "Dispatches them with brutal efficiency... The violence is impactful because it's rare; she doesn't enjoy killing, but she's very good at it." **LLM Tropes Here:** - "Brutal efficiency" is clean language for violence. It's efficient, it's brutal—it's almost clinical. - The explanation "because it's rare" is meta. It's the writer explaining why violence should feel impactful, rather than making it impactful. - "She doesn't enjoy killing" is moral hedging. We're assured she's not a monster. **How to Make It More Human:** - Make the violence uglier. Less "brutal efficiency," more specific. She breaks something. There's a sound. There's a moment where it doesn't go according to plan and she has to adapt. It's not clean. - Maybe she does enjoy it—at least some part of her. Maybe that's what frightens her about herself. - Don't explain why violence should feel impactful. Just write it so it does. ### The Structure (Eight Chapters with "Purposes") Each chapter has a stated purpose: "Establish character," "The inversion," "Expand the scope," "Escalation," etc. **LLM Tropes Here:** - This is over-structuring. Each chapter has a function, which means each chapter is engineered. - The purposes are generic: "establish character," "expand the scope." These could apply to any story. - There's no sense of the chapters being organic—they're building blocks in a machine. **How to Make It More Human:** - Let chapters have accidental purposes. Maybe one chapter goes somewhere unexpected because the writer got interested in something. - Don't state the purpose—let readers discover it. Don't tell us "This chapter exists to show her capability beyond combat"—just write a chapter where she demonstrates capability beyond combat. - Maybe some chapters are shorter, some longer. Maybe there's a chapter that's just one long scene. Maybe the structure isn't perfect. ### The Ending "She puts down the paper and goes to check on her new charge." **LLM Tropes Here:** - This is a bow. The cycle continues, which is slightly subverted, but it's still neat. She escapes, she continues her work. - The epilogue is too resolved. "Emma is safe. Other children will be safe." - It's pleasant. It's agreeable. It doesn't leave a bad taste. **How to Make It More Human:** - Don't make it so neat. Maybe she's caught. Maybe she doesn't get away with it. Maybe there's real cost. - Maybe the ending is ambiguous—we don't know if she'll keep doing this or if she's done. Maybe she's not sure either. - Maybe it's darker. Maybe the cycle isn't noble—maybe it's obsessive, maybe it's unhealthy, maybe she's not a guardian but something more tragic. - Don't wrap up what happens to the network. Leave some of them out there. Let Mara's victory be incomplete. ### The Voice "Third-person limited, following the protagonist. Prose will be punchy and direct—short sentences, vivid imagery, minimal interiority." **LLM Tropes Here:** - Third-person limited is the default. It's safe. - "Punchy and direct" is the pulp default, which means it's the expected style. There's no risk. - "Minimal interiority" avoids the complex inner life that makes characters feel real. **How to Make It More Human:** - Try a different POV. First person. Maybe the child narrates (unreliable, incomplete). Maybe it's close third but sometimes shifts to Emma's perspective. - Let the prose breathe differently in different chapters. Maybe the violent chapters are spare, the reflective chapters are more lush. - Give Mara interiority even if it's minimal—let us have glimpses into what she's thinking, not just what she's doing. ### The "Hedging" in the Plan Itself Looking at my intentions: "She babysits because she needs something normal, something innocent." "She's skilled, controlled, and terrifyingly competent—but she has a line." "The violence should be impactful because it's rare." These are all hedged, balanced, qualified statements. They're designed to be acceptable. A human writer would make bolder, less careful choices. --- ## Brainstorming: Ideas to Make It More Human ### Make Mara More Frightening (In All the Right Ways) - She has moments where she loses control—where the violence is more than necessary - She's not always in control; sometimes she's driven by something she doesn't understand - Her protectiveness toward children might be not entirely healthy—it's obsessive, it's consuming - She's not a hero; she's something closer to a weapon that doesn't know how to be anything else ### Give the Antagonists Specificity and Texture - Name the leader. Make him someone we know—not just "Carver the criminal." Give him a personality, a history, something that makes him not just a function. - Maybe one of the intruders is young, in over his head. Maybe he tries to run. Maybe she lets him—or doesn't. - Show what they do in detail—not just "they're criminals," but this particular thing, this specific operation, the thing that pushes Mara past her line. ### Complicate Emma - She sees the violence. She doesn't just "witness" it—she's changed by it. Maybe she has nightmares. Maybe she starts acting out. - She asks Mara questions that Mara can't answer. "Did you kill them?" "Did you enjoy it?" - Maybe Emma is not a perfect victim/protector-motivation. Maybe she's difficult, angry, has her own trauma that Mara doesn't know how to handle. ### Break the Structure - Maybe some chapters are out of order - Maybe some events happen off-page—we hear about them, we don't see them - Maybe one chapter is just a conversation, another is just action, no "purpose" beyond what the scene is - Maybe it doesn't take 72 hours—maybe it's compressed into one night, or stretched over weeks ### Make the Ending Ambivalent or Dark - She doesn't get away with it. She's arrested, or wounded, or something is lost that can't be recovered. - The revenge doesn't feel good. She wins and it's hollow. - She walks away but we don't know if she'll stop. Maybe she'll keep doing this forever. Maybe that's not heroic—maybe it's tragic. - Don't give us the epilogue with the newspaper. Let us wonder. ### Give It Texture - Specific details: the brand of cigarettes she smokes, the song on the radio when she drives, the smell of the house, the texture of the sheets - These details shouldn't all be functional—they should feel like the writer noticing things, caring about them - Ground it somewhere specific—a town, a region, a season, weather that matters ### Let the Voice Be Distinctive - Maybe she's more laconic—not "punchy" in the pulp way, but genuinely spare, someone who doesn't say much - Maybe there's dark humor—she makes jokes that aren't appropriate, that show how she's not quite normal - Maybe she has specific quirks, specific ways of being, that aren't explained—they just are ### Take Actual Risks - Maybe the child dies (not suggested by the prompt, but could happen) - Maybe she fails—the network wins, or she has to run, or something is taken from her that she can't get back - Maybe it's not really about the network at all—maybe it's about something more personal, something in her past that this triggers - Maybe she's not entirely reliable—she tells us one thing but we suspect another ### Avoid the "Noble Monster" Trope - She kills people. She enjoys some of it. Maybe that's the most human thing about her—the part that isn't noble. - She's not a hero. She doesn't want to be. She's not protecting children because it's right—she's doing it because she doesn't know how to do anything else. - The "line she won't cross" might be blurry, or it might not exist in the way we think. --- This critique and brainstorming is meant to identify where the plan might be falling into typical LLM patterns—safe writing, over-structure, hedging, resolution—and to generate ideas for making it more human, more distinctive, more alive. The next step would be to take these critiques and actually revise the plan, but for now, this is the landscape of what's there and what could be.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Formulated Intentions for "They Fucked with the Wrong Babysitter"** This story is a hardboiled pulp revenge tale that inverts the vulnerable babysitter trope—but it does so with genuine teeth. The violence will be rendered, not described. The protagonist will be frightening, not "terrifyingly competent" in a clean way. The ending will have weight and ambiguity, not resolution. **Plot Choice:** The story unfolds over one night and the following morning. Mara (late teens, early twenties—she's young, which makes her vulnerability and her capability both more shocking) takes a routine babysitting job at an isolated farmhouse. Three men come for the family's safe. They expect a terrified girl. They find something else. But this isn't just about one night—the men are connected to someone from Mara's past. Not her military past. Something worse. Something personal. The revenge isn't abstract; it's specific, and it costs her something. **Protagonist:** Mara is nineteen. She babysits because it's easy money and the hours keep her awake at night. She grew up in a small town. Her father taught her to fight—taught her too well. She left home at sixteen and never went back. She doesn't talk about herself. She doesn't explain. When the men come, we see what she can do, and it's not clean, and she doesn't enjoy it, but she's good at it. Or maybe she does enjoy part of it, and that's the thing she doesn't look at too closely. **Antagonists:** Three men, but they're not the main threat. They're hired muscle for someone named Dale Kessler—a man Mara knows. He lived two houses down from her when she was a child. He was kind to her. He was also doing something in the basement that she didn't understand then but understands now. He left town when she was twelve. She's been waiting. She's been ready. This is not the first time she's killed for him, but it will be the last. **The Child:** A boy, Eli, aged eight. He's difficult. He doesn't sleep well. He has nightmares. He asks too many questions. When he sees what Mara does to the intruders, he doesn't反应 the way a child "should." He watches. He doesn't scream. He says nothing for a long time. Then: "Are you going to kill him too? The man who sent them?" Mara doesn't answer. **Tone & Style:** First-person, Mara's voice. Sparse, direct, not performing. She doesn't narrate her own emotions—she describes what's around her, what's happening, what needs to be done. When she does feel something, it's small, it surprises her. The prose is shorter than expected. Fewer adjectives. More nouns.
